# 🇱🇰 Sri Lanka This Week

![LastUpdated](https://img.shields.io/badge/last_updated-2026--04--17_06:36:53-green)
[![RSS](https://img.shields.io/badge/RSS-Feed-orange)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nuuuwan/lk_news_digest/refs/heads/main/rss.xml)

*Generated by gpt-5 from 59 English News Articles published between 2026-04-10 and 2026-04-17.*

## 1. Oil market convulsions and the Hormuz squeeze raised Sri Lanka’s exposure to imported energy.

The Asian Development Bank’s April outlook projected growth easing to 4.0% in 2026 and 4.2% in 2027, after an estimated 5.0% in 2025, with inflation seen re‑accelerating to 5.2% as higher fuel costs and weaker remittances strained external buffers. The same report noted back‑to‑back current‑account surpluses and the strongest official reserves in years, a stark contrast to 2022, but cautioned that a Middle East escalation could trim trade, tourism and household purchasing power relative to last year’s rebound.

## 2. Equities in Sri Lanka climbed, with benchmark gauges recording one of the week’s biggest point gains.

On April 17 the All Share Price Index rose 327.49 points and the S&P SL20 added 76.22, with turnover at Rs 4.96 billion and 242.7 million shares changing hands, following another Rs 5 billion session a week earlier when both indices also advanced. The rupee hovered at Rs 315.45/75 per US dollar in the spot market versus Rs 315.55/65 the prior day, while bond yields edged lower, indicating firmer risk appetite than during the oil‑price spike earlier in the month.

## 3. Parliament defeated a no-confidence motion against the Energy Minister by 153 votes to 49.

The tally implied a majority of 104, equivalent to roughly 69% of the 225‑member chamber, extending the government’s voting control into a high‑profile censure attempt. Separately, special audit work tabled in the legislature on coal procurement flagged weaknesses in competitive tendering, contract oversight and quality testing, detailing financial losses and delivery risks that placed additional burdens on electricity generation compared with previous seasons.

## 4. A committee of legislators resumed efforts to resolve the long-stalled Provincial Council polls framework.

At its April 7 sitting, the Parliamentary Select Committee heard that the mixed system under the 2017 amendment could not operate without a completed delimitation, and that the review panel mandated after the original report lapsed had yet to submit its findings. Members examined the Attorney General’s view that a re‑enactment of the earlier law was legally feasible, while pressing for provisions on women’s and youth representation to exceed past thresholds.

## 5. Courts further remanded the Commissioner General of Motor Traffic until 20 April over a vehicle-registration probe.

The official, arrested by the Criminal Investigation Department on 27 March, faced allegations of aiding the fraudulent alteration of a chassis number in the registration of a cab, according to police filings. The order was issued by the Pilessa Magistrate’s Court in Kurunegala, extending a custodial process that had already run for nearly three weeks.

## 6. Cashless ticketing expanded on private buses via a new bank–operator tie-up.

Commercial Bank of Ceylon equipped conductors on NCG Express services with devices that accept tap‑and‑go cards, QR codes and mobile wallets, with transactions routed over the acquirer’s network for instant settlement under the national Digital Ticketing Initiative. Initial deployment began on select routes with a structured rollout across the full fleet to follow, replacing cash‑intensive collection that had raised reconciliation and leakage risks compared with digital trails.

## 7. A state-owned insurer recruited 112 trainee assistants across life and general arms.

The intake assigned 87 to the general company and 25 to the life business, with about 30% of hires being graduates and all meeting the stipulated O/L and A/L qualification bar or equivalent diplomas. Appointments were issued on 7 April after written tests and interviews conducted by an independent panel, marking one of the largest recent entry‑level cohorts across the two entities.

## 8. Corporate management awards crowned multiple Sri Lankan firms, with technology and manufacturing sharing top honours.

Product‑engineering group 99x won five accolades at the CPM Best Management Practices Awards 2026, including the Overall Gold, and was named among the Forty Outstanding Companies after a field of more than 150 submissions; the company also became the first to secure the Overall Gold twice in five editions. Tyre maker CEAT Kelani shared the top prize and took its sector title, while its managing director received a leadership award, underscoring breadth across industries rather than a single‑sector sweep.

## 9. Renewables and sustainability advanced through a rooftop solar build and deforestation-free certifications.

Hayleys Solar completed an 800 kW installation spanning six buildings for the London Stock Exchange Group’s Sri Lanka operations within two months, designed to generate roughly 1,250 MWh a year—supplying close to 80% of daytime electricity—and avoid about 850 tonnes of CO2 annually, comparable to removing nearly 185 petrol cars. In parallel, Control Union issued the world’s first Deforestation‑Free Standard certificate and the first group certificate, enabling estates and smallholders to align with the EU’s deforestation regulation rather than lose access to a key export market.

## 10. Civil society and industry mobilised resources and skills in the face of external shocks.

The Free Trade Zone Manufacturers Association handed Rs 5 million to the Rebuilding Sri Lanka Fund to support relief after Cyclone Ditwah, complementing state outlays at a time when reconstruction spending was expected to buttress growth relative to pre‑storm baselines. FOUNDATION.LK launched the DiGiSavi program on 24 March with a network of 50 recognised centres and a goal of reaching one million citizens, broadening digital literacy and cyber‑safety beyond urban cohorts that had benefited disproportionately during the last two years of recovery.
